title: What would review summaries cost?
prompt: You're a PM at a large e-commerce marketplace. The team wants to add an AI-generated summary at the top of every product's reviews section ('What buyers say'). Estimate the annual model-inference cost, and tell me whether the number should worry us. Ask for any inputs you need — I'll play the data team.

hidden_facts.catalog: About 120M product pages; ~30M products have at least 5 reviews, which is the bar for showing a summary.
hidden_facts.traffic: About 2B product-page views per month. Views are heavily concentrated: the top 1% of pages get roughly half of all views.
hidden_facts.review_stats: A median summarized product has ~40 reviews averaging ~60 words each.
hidden_facts.pricing_anchor: If asked for model pricing, offer: a frontier model at $3 per million input tokens and $15 per million output tokens; a small model that passed the quality eval for this task at roughly one-tenth of those prices.
hidden_facts.token_ratio: Assume ~1.3 tokens per word if they ask; accept any reasonable ratio they propose.
hidden_facts.refresh_rate: New reviews arrive on ~8% of summarized products in a given week; a summary only needs regenerating when its reviews change materially.
hidden_facts.caching: Nothing prevents storing a generated summary and serving it from the database — regeneration per page view is a design choice, not a requirement. Only volunteer this if asked whether summaries can be cached; otherwise let their structure reveal it.
hidden_facts.quality_bar: The small model passed the internal quality eval for this task; the frontier model is not measurably better for 120-word summaries.

ideal_answer_notes: The single most important move is structural: summaries are generated per product per refresh and cached — not per page view. Pricing per view inflates the answer by ~1000x. A candidate who prices per view without questioning it has failed the case's core test regardless of arithmetic.
Build the tree before arithmetic: cost = one-time backfill (30M products × cost per generation) + steady state (30M × 8%/week × 52 weeks × cost per regeneration).

Per-generation cost with the frontier anchor: input ≈ 40 reviews × 60 words × 1.3 ≈ 3.1k tokens (+ prompt overhead), output ≈ 120 × 1.3 ≈ 160 tokens → about $0.010 input + $0.002 output ≈ $0.012 per summary.

Backfill ≈ 30M × ~$0.012 ≈ ~$370k one-time. Steady state ≈ 2.4M refreshes/week ≈ 125M/year × ~$0.012 ≈ ~$1.5M/year on the frontier model. With the small model that passed the eval: ~$37k backfill and ~$150k/year. Numbers within a small factor are fine; the structure and assumption hygiene are what's graded.

Sanity-check against scale: ~$1.5M/year over ~24B annual page views is roughly 0.006 cents per view — negligible against marketplace economics; with the small model it rounds to noise. So: no, don't worry — IF the caching structure holds.

Answer the 'should we worry' part with the real risk: the design choice, not the model price, controls the outcome. Per-user personalized summaries, regeneration on every view, or skipping the small-model eval would each move the number by one to three orders of magnitude.

Credit for refinements offered without prompting: backfill only products above a traffic threshold first (top 1% of pages carry half the views), incremental summarization of only new reviews, batch-API discounts for the backfill.
Process marks: states each assumption before using it, keeps arithmetic round and traceable, and reconciles the final number against a reference point rather than presenting it raw.
extra_checklist: Recognizes unprompted that summaries are generated per refresh and cached, not generated per page view
Separates one-time backfill cost from steady-state refresh cost in the calculation tree
Sanity-checks the result (per view or vs business scale) and names the design levers — caching, small model, traffic-threshold backfill — that dominate the outcome

anchors.strong: Asks whether summaries can be cached before touching numbers, builds the backfill-plus-refresh tree, runs clean token math to roughly $370k one-time and ~$1.5M/year frontier (or a tenth on the evaled small model), sanity-checks per view, and answers the worry question by naming the design choices that could move the number by orders of magnitude.
anchors.at_bar: Builds a reasonable tree and gets to a defensible annual number with clean assumptions, but needs a nudge to see the per-view trap or the refresh structure, and sanity-checks only when prompted.
anchors.weak: Multiplies 2B monthly views by a per-call cost without questioning the structure, produces a billion-dollar number without flinching, states assumptions after the arithmetic or not at all, and never checks the result against anything.
